books.title: Too Much, and Nothing Left
books.subtitle: For the parent who runs every lunchbox and pickup on time and can't feel a single one of them — when the load stopped feeling heavy and started feeling like nothing at all
books.desc: There is a kind of depression that doesn't arrive as sadness — it arrives as a flat grey hum after years of holding everything at once. This is a book for the parent who is still doing it all and can't feel any of it. It won't ask you to do more; it will help you understand the numbness that came when there was no room left to feel.
books.long_desc:
It doesn't look like depression from the outside. The lunches get made. You answer the emails, you show up at pickup, you keep the whole quiet weather of the household moving. But somewhere along the way the load stopped feeling heavy and started feeling like nothing at all — a flat grey hum where the rest of you used to be. You tell people you're tired. Tired isn't the word. The word is closer to gone.

This book is for the parent who has been at capacity for so long that capacity became the whole sky. The one who can't point to a single catastrophe, only an unrelenting too-much that never let up long enough to be felt — the second job that begins when the first one ends, the night-waking, the mental list that never empties, the years of being needed without a single hour that was only yours. When too much arrives for long enough, the mind does something quiet and protective: it stops letting things land. The numbness you're calling failure is the flood, gone still.

In these pages you'll come to recognize how overwhelm becomes flatness — how a self that has run at a flood line for years finally lets the water go grey and motionless, not because you're weak but because feeling everything was no longer survivable. You'll see how the grey is not the absence of love for your children; it is the cost of carrying that love with nothing underneath it. And you'll find what it means to set one corner of the weight down, for one honest hour, without abandoning a single person who needs you.

This book will not optimize your mornings. It will not hand you a routine, a gratitude practice, or one more thing to do well. There is no program here to fall behind on. It will not tell you that you're lucky and should feel it. The flatness is not a character flaw to fix; it is information about a load no one was ever meant to carry alone, and information can be listened to instead of beaten.

Nothing here asks you to feel more than you can. That's the point. You're allowed to be this empty and still be a good parent. You're allowed to put the weight down for the length of one chapter and pick up only what is actually yours.

Book 1 of the Stillness Press depression series.
books.angle: You didn't burn out loud. You just went quiet.
books.author: Sam Meridian

books.title: Still Scanning at Midnight
books.subtitle: For the parent who lies awake listening for the cough, the late text, the next thing about to go wrong — when years of never letting your guard down have hollowed out into grey
books.desc: Some depression is what's left after years of never being able to switch off. When a parent's mind is always scanning — for the cough, the late text, the thing about to go wrong — that endless vigilance eventually hollows out into a grey, depleted flatness. This book is for the watcher who keeps everyone safe and is quietly vanishing inside the watching.
books.long_desc:
The house is quiet. Everyone is breathing. And still, at midnight, your mind is awake and listening — for the cough that means a fever, the thing your kid said that you keep turning over, the budget, the next small thing in the dark that could go wrong. You've been listening like this so long you don't remember how to let your attention rest. Even sleep feels like a risk you can't quite afford. So you keep listening, and somewhere underneath all that watchfulness, the light in you goes dim.

This book is for the parent whose mind never quite settles. The one who became the person who notices first and never got to hand it over — the one who anticipates everything, holds every contingency, keeps one ear open through the whole night, and pays for it with a flatness that has no off switch. What looks like depression here is often the long cost of staying braced: an attention that has been held tight against the dark for so many years that, eventually, it simply goes grey. You cannot stay this watchful forever. When you finally can't hold the watching anymore, it doesn't hand you peace. It hands you the flat grey hum.

In these pages you'll come to recognize how the watching became your resting state, and why the part of you that listens for danger genuinely believes the family will come apart the moment you close your eyes. You'll see how vigilance and depression are not opposites but a sequence — how the same attentiveness that kept everyone safe is the attentiveness now wearing you thin. And you'll find what it might mean to set the listening down for the length of one hour, and discover that the thing you were braced against does not, in fact, arrive the second you stop bracing.

This book will not hand you a calming routine to perfect or tell you to simply trust that everything will be fine. It will not optimize your sleep or shame your worry. It will not turn letting go into one more discipline you have to master. There is no program here, because the watchful part of you does not need another task — it needs to be told, gently and truthfully, that it is allowed to rest.

Nothing here will promise you that nothing bad will ever happen. That's the point. But you were never meant to be the only thing standing between your family and the dark. You're allowed to set the watching down for an hour and find that the lamp stays lit without you holding it.

Book 3 of the Stillness Press depression series.

books.title: The Highlight Reel at the School Gate
books.subtitle: For the parent who measures a hard, ordinary day against the polished version everyone posts at pickup and on the feed — and keeps coming up short
books.desc: At pickup, in the group chat, on the feed, everyone else seems to be doing this with a grace you can't find in yourself. This is a book for the parent who is always quietly behind some invisible standard, and who has turned a sharp, capable mind against the one person it should be protecting. It won't help you finally measure up. That's the point.
books.long_desc:
You see it everywhere. The other parent at drop-off who looks rested and unhurried. The feed full of birthday parties you'd never have the time or money to pull off. The friend who somehow works, cooks, exercises, and answers texts within the hour. And you, watching from inside your own tired life, doing the math you always do -- and coming out behind.

This is a book for the working parent who measures. The one who can't see another family without ranking yours against it. Who reads someone else's ease as a quiet indictment of your own struggle. Who knows, somewhere, that comparison is a trap, and still walks into it a dozen times a day because the alternative -- just being where you are, as you are -- feels too exposed.

We'll slow down and look at what comparison actually is. Not a clear-eyed assessment, but a sleight of hand: you hold up the most polished surface of someone else's life against the unedited inside of your own, and call the gap a truth about your worth. We'll notice how it feeds on a scrolling mind, how it makes strangers into rivals you never agreed to compete with, how it turns a genuinely capable parent into someone who feels perpetually a step behind a finish line that keeps moving.

Nothing here will help you win. There's no system to finally pull ahead, no routine that makes you the calm one others measure themselves against. The wanting-to-win is the wound, and we're not feeding it. We're setting it down.

What you'll come to recognize instead is how much of your life you've been watching from the outside, narrating it as a competition you're failing, when it was only ever yours to live. You'll feel the difference between admiration that opens something and comparison that closes it. You'll find that your worth was never a position in a ranking -- it doesn't rise when you're ahead or fall when you're behind, because it was never on that scale at all.

The other parents are not the proof of anything. They're just people, doing this the only way they know how, probably measuring themselves against someone too. You can set the measuring down. You can stop pointing the sharpest part of your mind at yourself, in the dark, and let your own ordinary, sufficient life simply be the one you're in.

Book 2 of the Stillness Press self worth series.

books.title: The Part of You That Thinks It's Failing
books.subtitle: For the parent who does everything and still lies awake feeling like none of it counts -- a quiet look at the shame underneath the to-do list
books.desc: There is a quiet voice that follows you through the day, keeping a tally of every way you fell short as a parent, a partner, a person. This is a book for the part of you that decided, somewhere along the way, that you yourself are the problem. It will not hand you another way to fix that. It will sit with you while you finally set it down.
books.long_desc:
You got everyone where they needed to go today. You answered the emails, packed the bag you forgot last week, made dinner out of almost nothing. And still, lying in the quiet at the end of it, there's a feeling you can't quite shake -- that it wasn't enough, that you weren't enough, that if anyone really saw how you were holding it all together they'd see the cracks.

This is a book for the parent carrying shame they would never call by that name. The one who reads every shortfall as evidence of something wrong, not with the situation, but with them. You overdo it so no one will notice. You apologize for needs you haven't even named. You measure yourself against a version of you that was never possible, and you lose, quietly, every single day.

Here we slow down enough to see what's actually happening. Shame is not the truth about you. It's a story you absorbed -- from a childhood, a culture, a moment you barely remember -- that fused who you are with how much you produce. We'll look at how it whispers, how it borrows your own voice, how it disguises itself as responsibility so you'll never think to question it. We'll notice the difference between the honest ache of falling short on a hard day and the older, deeper conviction that you are the thing that needs fixing.

Nothing here will optimize you. There is no morning routine, no mindset upgrade, no checklist that finally makes you worthy. That's the whole point. The belief that you have to earn your way into being enough is exactly the thing we're setting down -- not perfecting, not managing, setting down.

What you'll find instead is a kind of company. The slow recognition that the part of you that thinks it's failing has been working overtime to protect you, and that it can finally rest. That worth isn't a score you climb toward on your best days and fall from on your worst. It's the ground you've been standing on the whole time, even now, even tired, even unsure.

You don't have to become someone else to deserve a moment of peace. You can stop bracing against your own life long enough to feel that it was never the proof of your failure. It was just a life, lived by someone doing their best.

Let this be where you put it down.

books.title: Everyone Else Looks Like They've Got It
books.subtitle: For the working parent measuring a body full of strain against everyone who seems to manage it with ease
books.desc: Everyone else seems to be managing it with ease, and you're measuring your strained, tired body against theirs. This is a book for the working parent who always comes up short in the comparison — and the relief of finally putting the yardstick down.
books.long_desc:
You see them at drop-off, in the meeting, on the screen — the other parents who seem to have it handled. Calm. Capable. Unbothered by the weight you can feel in your own chest right now. And something in you measures: their ease against your strain, their put-together morning against the one you barely survived, their steady bodies against yours that won't stop bracing. You always come up short. You've been keeping this scorecard so long you've forgotten you're the one holding the pen.

This book is for the working parent who lives in comparison. The one who scrolls and shrinks, who watches everyone else seem fine and reads it as evidence of their own failure. Who carries the quiet exhaustion of never measuring up to people whose insides they cannot actually see.

Here you'll come to recognize what comparison is doing to your nervous system — the way each measurement tightens the body, confirms the deficit, and sends you reaching for more effort to close a gap that was never real. You'll feel where the comparing lands: the sink in the stomach, the held breath, the bracing that comes the instant you stack yourself against someone else. You'll learn that the calm you envy is mostly a surface you're comparing your depths to, and that the way out is not to finally win — not to become impressive enough to stop feeling less-than — but to turn the instrument off its endless target: yourself, found wanting. The body cannot settle while it's being measured. It settles when the measuring stops.

Nothing here will help you win the comparison. That's the point. This is not a book that will make you more put-together than the other parents or hand you the routine that finally closes the gap. There is nothing here to optimize, and no superior version of you waiting to come out on top.

What it offers instead is recognition — a language for the comparing that you can reach for the next time it starts, at the school gate, in the feed, in the dark when everyone else seems asleep and fine and you are neither. You'll come to understand what your body does when it measures itself against everyone else, and why, given all you carry unseen, it makes complete sense.

Not fixed. Not finished. Just the scorecard set down at last, and a body that no longer has to win to be allowed to rest.

Book 7 of the Stillness Press somatic healing series.

books.title: Your Body Is Still Bracing
books.subtitle: For the working parent whose nervous system keeps scanning for a danger that already passed — and never quite stands down
books.desc: The kids are safe, the day is over, and your shoulders are still up by your ears. This is a book for the working parent whose body keeps sounding an alarm for something that isn't there — and a slow, kind way to let it finally stand down.
books.long_desc:
You're sitting in the quiet after the house has gone still, and your body is braced for something. You couldn't say what. There's no fire, no emergency, no child crying down the hall — and still your jaw is set, your breath is high and shallow, your stomach pulled tight around a threat that never arrives. You've been living this way so long it stopped feeling like a state and started feeling like you.

This book is for the working parent whose alarm never fully switches off. The one who feels the surge before the phone even buzzes. Who lies down exhausted and finds the body wide awake, scanning, certain that letting go would mean missing the thing that finally goes wrong.

Here you'll come to recognize what your nervous system is actually doing — that the bracing was never a flaw, but an old protection that learned to fire early and never learned it could stop. You'll feel, slowly, the difference between a real alarm and the echo of one. You'll learn what it is in the body that keeps the alarm lit, and what it takes for it to believe, at last, that the danger has passed. Not by forcing calm. Not by talking yourself down one more time at 3am. By turning toward the sensation instead of away, and letting it move the way it always wanted to.

This is not a book that will make the feeling stop on command. It won't optimize your stress response or hand you a morning routine to perfect. There is nothing here to get right, and no calmer version of you to finally become. The alarm is not the enemy, and neither are you.

What it offers instead is recognition — a language for what is happening inside you, that you can reach for the next time the surge comes, on a subway platform or between two meetings or in the dark with everyone else asleep. You'll come to understand what your body is doing and why, after everything, it makes complete sense.

Not fixed. Not finished. Just no longer alone with it, and no longer at war with the part of you that was only ever trying to keep you safe.

books.angle: Nothing is wrong. Your body hasn't heard yet.

books.title: Something Was Lost in the Rush
books.subtitle: For the working parent carrying an unnamed grief — the loss underneath the anxiety, waiting under all the noise to finally be felt
books.desc: Underneath the anxiety, something is missing — a loss you never had time to mourn. This is a book for the working parent carrying an unnamed grief in the body, and a way to let it move at last.
books.long_desc:
Some nights it isn't fear underneath the restlessness. It's something heavier, quieter — a sense of something missing that you can't quite name. The self you were before all this. The closeness you meant to keep. The version of this life you imagined, or the people and seasons that slipped past while you were holding everything together. You've been calling it anxiety because anxiety has a shape you can manage. But the body knows the difference, and the body has been waiting for you to feel what it's actually carrying.

This book is for the working parent carrying grief they never had room to mourn. The one who lost something — a person, a possibility, a former life — in the rush of providing and parenting and keeping on, and never got the pause to lay it down. The one whose anxiety, looked at closely, turns out to be loss with nowhere to go.

Here you'll come to recognize how unprocessed grief lives in the body and disguises itself as everyday dread. You'll feel where the loss has settled — the heaviness in the chest, the ache behind the eyes you keep blinking back, the tightness that turns out to be held tears. You'll learn that grief is not a problem to be solved but a movement that needs to be allowed; that what was never mourned doesn't disappear, it waits, pressing up as restlessness until it's finally felt. Not by getting over it. Not by being strong about it one more time. By making room, at last, for the body to grieve the way it has needed to all along — slowly, in waves, with nothing required of you but to let it move.

This is not a book that will take the loss away or teach you to leave it behind on schedule. It will not optimize your grieving or hand you stages to complete. There is nothing here to get right, and no healed-over version of you who never has to feel it again.

What it offers instead is recognition — a language for the grief beneath the anxiety that you can reach for the next time the heaviness rises, on the commute, after the children are asleep, in the quiet that finally lets the missing thing be felt. You'll come to understand what your body has been carrying, and why, given all you never had time to mourn, it makes complete sense.

Not fixed. Not finished. Just no longer alone with the loss, and a body allowed, at last, to let it move through.

Book 6 of the Stillness Press somatic healing series.
books.angle: You're not anxious. You're grieving something you never had room to mourn.
books.author: Sam Meridian

books.title: There Is No Room Left
books.subtitle: For the working parent carrying more than one body can hold — when everything arrives at once and there's nowhere to put it down
books.desc: It all arrived at the same time and there was nowhere to put it. This is a book for the working parent who is past full — whose body is flooded long before the mind admits it — and a way back to a little ground beneath your feet.
books.long_desc:
It came all at once, the way it always does. The work and the school email and the small body needing to be held and the thing you forgot and the thing you can't forget — and somewhere underneath it your own body, flooded, with nowhere left to put any of it down. You're not thinking anymore so much as bracing against the next wave. There is no room. There hasn't been room in a long time.

This book is for the working parent who lives past full. The one whose capacity gave out hours ago and who is still going, because going is the only option anyone left on the table. Who feels the flood rise in the chest and the throat and pushes through it, again, because the day does not pause for a nervous system that has had enough.

Here you'll come to recognize overwhelm for what it actually is in the body — not a failure of willpower or organization, but a system that has taken on more than it can metabolize and has no moment to discharge it. You'll feel the difference between adding one more strategy and finally letting the water find a level. You'll learn where the flood lives in you, what it's like to let a single wave move through instead of damming it, and how even a breath of space can return when there seemed to be none. Not by doing more. Not by becoming more efficient at being overwhelmed. By stopping, for a moment, and letting the body catch up to a life it's been chasing.

This is not a book that will give you your time back or teach you to do it all without strain. It will not optimize your overwhelm or hand you a system to manage the unmanageable. There is nothing here to perfect, and no tireless version of you waiting on the other side of the right routine.

What it offers instead is a place to set something down — a language for the flood that you can reach for the next time it rises, in the car before you go in, between two meetings, in the kitchen at 9pm with the dishes still waiting. You'll come to understand what your body is doing when it's overwhelmed, and why, given everything you carry, it makes complete sense.

Not fixed. Not finished. Just a little more room than there was, and the knowing that the ground is still there underneath all of it.

Book 2 of the Stillness Press somatic healing series.
books.angle: Too much, all at once, and no room to set any of it down.
books.author: Sam Meridian

books.title: The Thing You Tense Up to Hide
books.subtitle: For the working parent overworking to cover a defect no one else can see — and the quiet contraction in the body that gives it away
books.desc: There's a thing you've decided is wrong with you, and you've spent years overworking to cover it. This is a book for the working parent who carries that shame as a tightness in the body — and a way to let the contraction, at last, soften.
books.long_desc:
There's a moment your body knows before your mind does — the small contraction the instant you're about to be seen too closely. A tightening in the chest, a held breath, a turning-inward to cover the thing you've decided is wrong with you. You've spent years overworking to hide it: being the parent who has it together, the one who never drops the ball, the one no one would suspect carries a quiet certainty of not being enough. The hiding has become so much a part of you that you feel it in your shoulders.

This book is for the working parent who lives with shame. Not guilt over a thing you did, but the deeper, older belief that something is wrong with you — that if anyone saw underneath the competence, they'd see it too. The one who performs okayness all day and feels the contraction every time the mask slips.

Here you'll come to recognize shame for what it actually is in the body — not the truth about you, but a contraction that learned to fold you inward and call it safety. You'll feel where it grips: the collapse in the chest, the eyes that drop, the breath that goes shallow the moment you're exposed. You'll learn what shame does to a nervous system, and what it takes for the contraction to loosen — not by proving yourself worthy, not by working hard enough to finally earn the okayness, but by bringing warmth to the very part of you that has only ever known it should be hidden. The body doesn't release shame by being argued out of it. It releases when it is met without flinching.

This is not a book that will fix you, because you were never broken. It will not optimize your self-worth or hand you affirmations to recite over a feeling that doesn't believe words. There is nothing here to perform, and no flawless version of you to finally become acceptable.

What it offers instead is recognition — a language for the contraction that you can reach for the next time it grips you, in a meeting where you feel exposed, at the school gate, in the dark with the day's small failures replaying. You'll come to understand what your body does when shame moves through it, and why, given all you've carried alone, it makes complete sense.

Not fixed. Not finished. Just a little more room to be seen, and a body learning that the thing it has been hiding was never the problem.

Book 5 of the Stillness Press somatic healing series.
books.angle: You've been hiding it so long you can feel it in your shoulders.
books.author: Sam Meridian

books.title: One Thought, and Then a Thousand
books.subtitle: For the working parent caught in the loop — when a single worry tightens into a spiral the body can't think its way out of
books.desc: It starts with a single worry and tightens into a thousand. This is a book for the working parent whose mind loops back to the same thought at 3am — and a way out that the body finds when the thinking can't.
books.long_desc:
It begins with one thought. A small one, sometimes — something you said, something you didn't, the thing that might go wrong with the kids, the work, the future you can't see. And then it pulls. One thought becomes the next becomes a thousand, each one tightening the last, the whole spiral feeding on itself until your chest is small and your breath is short and you are somewhere far from the room you're actually in. You've tried to think your way out. The thinking is the trap.

This book is for the working parent who spirals. The one who lies down bone-tired and watches the mind start its loop, returning again and again to the same worn groove. Who knows, in daylight, that the thought isn't true, and finds that knowing does nothing at all when the spiral starts to turn at 3am.

Here you'll come to recognize the spiral for what it actually is — not a thinking problem to be out-argued, but a body in distress reaching for the only lever it trusts, looping because the loop feels like doing something about a fear it doesn't know how to hold. You'll feel where the spiral grips you below the neck, in the tightening you usually try to ignore. You'll learn that the way out is not up through more thought but down into the body — that a spiral has to be met where it lives, in sensation, in breath, in the ground under you. Not by stopping the thoughts. Not by replacing them with better ones. By turning, gently, toward the tightening itself, and letting it loosen the way it can only loosen when it's finally felt.

This is not a book that will quiet your mind on command or teach you to control your thoughts. It will not optimize your rumination or hand you an affirmation to drown it out. There is nothing here to perfect, and no perpetually serene mind waiting once you learn the trick.

What it offers instead is a door the spiral can't follow you through — a language for the loop that you can reach for the next time it begins, between meetings, in the school pickup line, in the dark with the house asleep and the mind wide open. You'll come to understand what your body is doing when it spirals, and why, given all it's trying to manage, it makes complete sense.

Not fixed. Not finished. Just no longer trapped inside the loop alone, and a body that knows, now, there's a way down and out.

Book 4 of the Stillness Press somatic healing series.
books.angle: It starts with one thought. It never ends with one.
books.author: Sam Meridian

books.title: The Part of You That Never Sits Down
books.subtitle: For the working parent whose mind is always on watch — scanning the room, the faces, the future for a threat that never comes
books.desc: Some part of you is always scanning — the room, the faces, the next thing that could go wrong. This is a book for the working parent who can't put the watch down, and a slow way of letting the body believe it's finally safe enough to.
books.long_desc:
Even now, some part of you is watching. Reading the room. Checking the faces for what might be wrong, the calendar for what might go sideways, the small sounds in the house for the one that means you're needed. You can be sitting still and entirely on duty — a sentry that never got the message it could stand down. You don't remember choosing it. It just became the way you move through a day.

This book is for the working parent whose mind never goes off watch. The one who scans a playground for exits without meaning to, who feels the shoulders rise when a door opens, who lies down at night with the body still posted at the window of some old threat. Hypervigilance, someone may have called it. From the inside it just feels like the cost of being the one everyone depends on.

Here you'll come to recognize the watcher for what it is — not paranoia, not a personality flaw, but a nervous system that learned, early and well, that safety had to be earned by never looking away. You'll feel where the watching lives in your body: the set of the jaw, the eyes that won't soften, the breath held high in case. You'll learn what it takes for that part of you to trust, for even a moment, that it can rest — that the room can be held by something other than your constant attention. Not by forcing yourself to relax. Not by white-knuckling your way to calm. By offering the watcher, at last, the one thing it has never been given: a sense that the ground is steady, and that it is allowed to set the post down.

This is not a book that will switch off your vigilance or teach you to stop caring. It will not optimize your attention or hand you a discipline to finally master your own mind. There is nothing here to get right, and no effortlessly serene version of you waiting past the next technique.

What it offers instead is recognition — a language for the watcher that you can reach for the next time you catch yourself scanning, in a meeting, at the dinner table, in the dark when the house is finally quiet and your body still won't believe it. You'll come to understand what your body is doing when it can't stand down, and why, after everything it's held, it makes complete sense.

Not fixed. Not finished. Just a watcher that knows, now, it doesn't have to do this alone — and a body learning, slowly, what it feels like to be off duty.

Book 3 of the Stillness Press somatic healing series.
books.angle: Always scanning. Always on. Never quite off duty.
books.author: Sam Meridian
